input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin** system under study in the referenced **Living Lab Digital Twin** case study (Gerber et al., [33]) is a **research-oriented, LEED Platinum-certified academic building** integrated into a **living lab environment** (TwinLight/BOREALIS). The system comprises:
- **Core Components**:
  - **Structural and environmental sensors** (e.g., temperature, humidity, occupancy, air quality) for real-time condition monitoring.
  - **Building Management Systems (BMS)** for automated control and data aggregation.
  - **Interactive digital interfaces** enabling user engagement in research and operational testing.
- **Operational Domain**:
  - **Academic/research facility** with dual roles: (1) **real-world testing ground** for digital twin technologies and (2) **living lab** for interdisciplinary collaboration (e.g., engineering, architecture, sustainability).
  - **Life-cycle-encompassing focus**: Spans design, construction, occupancy, and maintenance phases, emphasizing **adaptive optimization** and **sustainability metrics** (e.g., energy efficiency, occupant comfort).
- **Key Characteristics**:
  - **Modular and extensible architecture** to accommodate evolving research objectives.
  - **High-fidelity sensor networks** for granular data collection, enabling **predictive analytics** and **condition-based maintenance**.
  - **Integration with external stakeholders** (e.g., researchers, policymakers) via **open-access data platforms**.
  - **Dynamic boundary conditions**: Physical system interacts bidirectionally with the digital twin for **closed-loop validation** of models and simulations.
  - **Compliance with industry standards** (e.g., LEED certification) to ensure relevance in **sustainable building practices**.
  - **Scalability**: Designed to support **multi-disciplinary experiments**, including **energy systems, structural health monitoring, and occupant behavior studies**.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** system provides **targeted services** to **Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, and DT Engineers** (as specified in Table I of the abstract), enabling **real-time monitoring, predictive analytics, and life-cycle optimization**. Key services include:

- **Real-Time Monitoring**:
  - **Continuous data ingestion** from IoT sensors (e.g., temperature, humidity, occupancy) via **Eclipse Hono/Ditto integration**.
  - **Streaming analytics** for immediate condition assessment (e.g., energy consumption trends, equipment health).
  - **Visualization dashboards** (e.g., **Unreal Engine-based interfaces**) for **interactive 3D exploration** of building states.

- **Predictive Analytics**:
  - **Time-series forecasting** of operational metrics (e.g., HVAC efficiency, structural stress) using **model-driven simulation engines**.
  - **Anomaly detection** via **statistical methods** (e.g., **control charts, moving averages**)—*not machine learning models like Isolation Forest*—to flag deviations from expected performance.
  - **Life-cycle-encompassing optimization**: Generates **what-if scenarios** for design adjustments, maintenance scheduling, and sustainability improvements.

- **Control and Automation**:
  - **Closed-loop feedback** between the DT and physical system for **adaptive control** of building systems (e.g., automated HVAC adjustments based on occupancy data).
  - **Rule-based automation** (e.g., energy-saving protocols) derived from **model-driven engineering (MDE) frameworks**.

- **Visualization and Collaboration**:
  - **Immersive 3D environments** (e.g., **Unreal Engine**) for **real-time collaboration** among stakeholders, enabling **virtual walkthroughs** and **interactive data exploration**.
  - **Augmented Reality (AR) overlays** to visualize **predictive maintenance alerts** or **energy-saving recommendations** directly on physical assets.

- **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) Support**:
  - **Automated generation of simulation models** from **domain-specific languages (DSLs)** or **ontologies** to ensure consistency between physical and digital representations.
  - **Prompt engineering frameworks** (e.g., **Impromptu**[40]) to **dynamically adapt DT models** based on evolving research questions or operational needs.

- **Reporting and Decision Support**:
  - **Automated compliance reports** (e.g., LEED certification tracking) generated via **model-driven analytics**.
  - **Trend analysis** for **long-term facility management planning**, including **predictive maintenance scheduling** and **resource allocation optimization**.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **Digital Twin implementation** leverages a **model-driven, open-source-centric architecture** to avoid the **proprietary constraints** of frameworks like **Eclipse BaSyx** or **Bentley iTwin.js**, which require **significant manual configuration**. Key enablers include:

- **Core Platforms and Protocols**:
  - **Eclipse Hono**: Acts as the **edge-to-cloud data ingestion layer**, standardizing **IoT device connectivity** via **MQTT/HTTP protocols** for sensor data acquisition.
  - **Eclipse Ditto**: Provides **device twin management** with **real-time synchronization** between physical and digital representations, supporting **event-driven updates** and **stateful interactions**.
  - **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) Tools**:
    - **Eclipse Modeling Framework (EMF)** and **Acceleo** for **automated code generation** from **UML/SysML models**.
    - **Kermeta** or **Xtend** for **domain-specific scripting** in simulation workflows.
  - **Simulation Engines**:
    - **Modelica-based simulators** (e.g., **Dymola**) for **multi-physics system modeling** (e.g., thermal, structural, fluid dynamics).
    - **Custom MDE pipelines** to **translate physical asset models** (e.g., BIM data) into **executable simulation models**.

- **Visualization and Interaction**:
  - **Unreal Engine 5**: Powers **high-fidelity, interactive 3D visualizations** with **real-time physics simulations** and **AR/VR support** for immersive DT exploration.
  - **Web-based dashboards** (e.g., **React.js + D3.js**) for **data visualization** and **alerting** (e.g., energy consumption heatmaps).

- **Data Management**:
  - **Apache Kafka** for **high-throughput, low-latency event streaming** between sensors, edge nodes, and the DT core.
  - **PostgreSQL + TimescaleDB** for **time-series data storage**, optimized for **querying historical trends** and **predictive analytics**.
  - **Ontology-based knowledge graphs** (e.g., **RDF/SPARQL**) to **semantically link** physical assets, sensors, and simulation models for **interoperability**.

- **Development and Integration**:
  - **GitLab CI/CD pipelines** for **automated model deployment** and **version control** of DT components.
  - **RESTful APIs** (e.g., **Spring Boot**) to expose DT services to **third-party applications** (e.g., facility management tools).
  - **Plug-in architecture** enabling **modular extension** of services (e.g., adding new sensor types or simulation modules).

- **Specialized Methodologies**:
  - **Model-Driven Prompt Engineering (MDPE)** (as referenced in [40]) to **dynamically generate DT queries** based on **natural language inputs** from users.
  - **Closed-loop validation frameworks** using **reinforcement learning** (mention in [33]) to **adapt simulation models** to real-world deviations.
  - **Automated test suites** (e.g., **JUnit + Modelio**) to verify **model consistency** between physical and digital twins.

- **Avoidance of Proprietary Dependencies**:
  - Explicit rejection of **Eclipse BaSyx** (due to **manual configuration overhead**) and **Bentley iTwin.js** (due to **proprietary ecosystem lock-in**).
  - **Open standards compliance**: Adherence to **OPC UA**, **ISO 15926**, and **BIM standards** (e.g., IFC) for **interoperability** with legacy systems.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system supports **real-time data exchange capabilities**, ensuring continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts with **low-latency requirements** to maintain operational relevance. The framework emphasizes **dynamic operational needs** over static BIM representations, enabling time scales for different services to adapt to **runtime configuration management**—allowing adjustments without full redeployment. While explicit synchronization frequencies (e.g., Hz or ms intervals) are not detailed, the system prioritizes **temporal granularity** sufficient for **immersive interactive visualization** and **facility monitoring/control**, aligning with the AECO domain’s demand for near-instantaneous feedback loops. Latency constraints are implicitly addressed via modular architecture and standardized extension points to accommodate evolving operational requirements without compromising temporal fidelity.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The system adopts a **centralized hub-and-spoke architecture**, where a **single Digital Twin Framework** acts as the primary orchestration layer. Three distinct **actor roles** are defined—*Building Control Engineers* (responsible for BIM-based device modeling and deployment), *Facility Managers* (interacting with the DT for real-time monitoring), and *DT Engineers* (extending capabilities via **custom service integration**)—but the document does not explicitly describe **parallel, non-conflicting operational twins** or decentralized instances. The modular architecture supports **standardized extension points** for scalable service deployment, though hierarchical multiplicities (e.g., multiple DT instances per building) are not addressed.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The Digital Twin system follows a **centralized hub-and-spoke architecture**, where the DT Framework acts as the **orchestration core** coordinating roles (Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, DT Engineers) and system components. The constellation is governed by **coordinated roles and system components** as illustrated in Figure 1, with the hub facilitating:
  - **Bidirectional data exchange** between physical assets and digital models.
  - **Modular service integration** via standardized extension points for custom analytics.
  - **Runtime configuration management** to dynamically adjust operational workflows without redeployment.
  The system’s **nonfunctional requirements (NF_X)** explicitly address **interoperability across heterogeneous systems**, **scalability** (from components to entire infrastructure), **maintainability** (low-overhead updates), and **accessibility** (user-friendly interfaces). Resource management is implied through the framework’s adaptability, though specific orchestration patterns (e.g., microservices, event-driven architectures) are not detailed beyond the modular design.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The framework integrates **Eclipse Hono** and **Ditto** for **real-time data exchange**, enabling seamless interoperability with external systems. While the document does not explicitly list **OPC UA** or other protocols, it emphasizes **standardized extension points** and **interoperability across heterogeneous systems** as core requirements. Dependencies on external systems are managed through the **centralized hub’s bidirectional data exchange**, though specific integration patterns (e.g., with other Digital Twins or enterprise systems) are not detailed.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin system leverages **BIM (Building Information Modeling)** as its foundational computational framework, integrating semantically rich, object-based 3D models that encapsulate geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics of building components and their interrelationships. The system employs **model-driven automation** (as per Design Science Research artifacts) to operationalize **Design-Time Models (DTMs)** and **Runtime Operational Models**, ensuring seamless synchronization between conceptual design phases and real-time operational control. Key model types include:
  - **Geometric Models**: High-fidelity 3D representations of building assets (e.g., structural elements, MEP systems) aligned with industry standards (e.g., IFC, COBie).
  - **Behavioral Models**: Simulated dynamic behaviors (e.g., HVAC performance, structural response) derived from parametric and physics-based simulations.
  - **Analytical Models**: Data-driven models (e.g., machine learning surrogates) for predictive analytics and anomaly detection.
  Data sources are integrated via **real-time synchronization** using **Eclipse Hono** (for IoT data ingestion) and **Ditto** (for device twin management), ensuring live updates from physical assets. Data management follows a **service-oriented architecture (SOA)** with modular extension points, enabling scalable integration of **AECO-specific data** (e.g., construction progress, maintenance logs). Model relationships are defined via **ontological mappings** (implicitly referenced in semantic integration requirements) to ensure consistency across design-time and runtime contexts. The framework supports **model-driven deployment** of operational control DTs, closing the gap between BIM-based design models and runtime operations through automated workflows (e.g., **R2: automated deployment process**).
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The Digital Twin system relies on **Eclipse Hono** and **Ditto** for **real-time data integration**, facilitating live synchronization between physical sensors and their digital representations. The infrastructure supports **runtime semantics**, including **sensor configurations**, but does not specify sensor types (e.g., temperature, motion), spatial distributions, or sampling frequencies. Data transmission is enabled via **Eclipse Hono’s messaging platform**, though the exact protocols (e.g., MQTT, CoAP) are not mentioned. The system focuses on **semantic integration** for live data exchange rather than post-processing for noise mitigation or error correction.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The Digital Twin system addresses **fidelity and validity** through **semantic integration** and **real-time connectivity**, ensuring alignment between physical assets and digital representations. Key considerations include:
  - **Model Fidelity Requirements**: High fidelity is achieved via **BIM’s semantically rich models**, which incorporate **geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics** of assets, validated against industry standards (e.g., IFC, COBie). The system prioritizes **operational control DTs**, where fidelity is critical for decision-making (e.g., predictive maintenance, energy optimization).
  - **Validation and Verification Methods**: Implicitly supported by **model-driven deployment artifacts** (e.g., **R2: automated deployment process**), which ensure reproducibility and consistency. **Design Science Research (DSR)** methodologies are applied to validate the framework’s ability to operationalize DTs in AECO contexts.
  - **Uncertainty Sources and Quantification**: While not explicitly quantified, uncertainties arise from **sensor noise, model simplifications, and temporal delays** in real-time data integration. Mitigation strategies include **real-time synchronization (Eclipse Hono + Ditto)** and **ontological mappings** to reduce semantic discrepancies.
  - **Error Detection and Correction Mechanisms**: The system’s **real-time data integration** (R3) enables continuous comparison between physical and digital states, facilitating **automated anomaly detection** (e.g., via Ditto’s device twin reconciliation). However, specific error correction protocols (e.g., feedback loops, adaptive models) are not detailed.
  - **Quality Assurance Processes**: Quality is ensured through **model-driven automation**, which standardizes deployment workflows and reduces manual intervention (R2). Additionally, the **modular architecture** (R4) allows for **service-level quality checks** at extension points, though formalized QA procedures (e.g., ISO/IEC standards) are not referenced.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in this Digital Twin (DT) framework is facilitated through **real-time data exchange** between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, ensuring **continuous synchronization** via a **modular architecture** with standardized extension points. The framework leverages **enriched BIM models** as the foundational data source, enabling automated extraction of domain semantics for operational monitoring. Data flows are managed through a **requirements-driven approach**, emphasizing **interoperability across heterogeneous systems** (NF_X) and **low-overhead updates** for maintainability. The system supports bidirectional data transmission, though specific data types (e.g., sensor readings, environmental metrics), communication protocols (e.g., exact protocols not detailed), or transmission frequencies are not explicitly mentioned in the provided documents. The framework prioritizes **data quality assurance** through its structured deployment process, where **Building Control Engineers** initiate deployment via BIM tools, ensuring alignment between physical assets and digital representations.

Key mechanisms include:
- **Bidirectional data exchange** enabled by the central DT Framework hub.
- **Runtime configuration management** for adaptability without full redeployment.
- **Automated semantic enrichment** of BIM models to support dynamic operational needs in the AECO domain.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The **virtual-to-physical interaction** in this Digital Twin framework is designed to enable **bidirectional communication** between the digital model and physical building systems, primarily for **real-time monitoring and control**. The framework supports **interactive visualization** and **control semantics** tailored to operational requirements, allowing **Facility Managers** to interact with the deployed DT for dynamic adjustments. While the documents emphasize the **bidirectional nature** of the interaction (e.g., via the TwinLight project context implied in the framework’s design), specific details such as **control message formats**, **validation/authentication mechanisms**, or **feedback systems** are not explicitly described. The framework’s **modular architecture** and **standardized extension points** facilitate custom service integration, enabling tailored control logic for physical assets. However, no mention of specific protocols (e.g., OPC UA, BACnet), command types, or conflict resolution strategies is provided in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The **technical network infrastructure** for this Digital Twin system is characterized by a **modular architecture** with **standardized extension points**, ensuring **interoperability across heterogeneous systems** (NF_X). The framework integrates **Eclipse Hono and Ditto** for **real-time data exchange**, though specific communication protocols (e.g., MQTT, CoAP, OPC UA), network topology (e.g., hybrid edge-cloud), or latency requirements are not detailed. The system prioritizes **scalability** (NF_X) and **reliability** through its **runtime configuration management**, enabling adjustments without full redeployment. Security protocols for network communication are implied via the framework’s **interoperability requirements**, but no explicit mention of encryption standards, firewalls, or redundancy mechanisms (e.g., failover systems) is present. The infrastructure supports **low-overhead updates** for maintainability, ensuring seamless integration of new services or analytics.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The **deployment infrastructure** for this Digital Twin system is governed by a **model-driven deployment framework**, enabling scalable DT generation from **enriched BIM models**. The system emphasizes **scalability** (NF_X) and **maintainability** through **low-overhead updates**, though specific hosting platforms (e.g., cloud, edge, on-premise) or deployment models (e.g., hybrid) are not explicitly mentioned. The framework leverages **runtime configuration management** to adapt to operational requirements without redeployment, ensuring flexibility. **Availability and reliability** are addressed via the framework’s **systematic foundation**, which bridges static BIM representations with dynamic operational needs. However, details such as **computational/storage requirements**, **scalability mechanisms** (e.g., auto-scaling), or **zero-downtime update capabilities** are not described in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle in this framework spans **five core phases**, each addressing the transition from static BIM representations to dynamic, operational DTs:

1. **Device Modeling**
   - Building Control Engineers embed **domain-specific control semantics** (sensors, actuators, controllers) directly into BIM models, overcoming BIM’s static limitations. This phase leverages **BIM enrichment** to create a **unified semantic foundation** for subsequent transformations. The process involves **model-driven engineering (MDE) principles** to define device configurations and control logic within the BIM environment.

2. **Model Transformation**
   - The framework employs **model-to-model (M2M) and model-to-text (M2T) transformations** to extract structured representations from enriched BIM models. These transformations generate **deployable DT artifacts** with minimal manual intervention, ensuring consistency between the physical and digital domains. The output includes **semantic annotations** for interoperability and **standardized data schemas** for runtime deployment.

3. **Runtime Deployment**
   - The DT is deployed as a **modular, real-time synchronized system** between physical infrastructure and its digital counterpart. This phase integrates **standardized extension points** (e.g., Eclipse Hono and Ditto) to enable seamless data exchange. The framework supports **continuous synchronization** via real-time APIs, ensuring operational coherence between the twin and physical assets.

4. **DT Extension**
   - DT Engineers extend functionality through **custom services and plugins**, integrated via the framework’s modular architecture. This phase enables **iterative improvement** of control policies (e.g., via the BOREALIS project) and supports **analytical extensions** (e.g., predictive maintenance, energy optimization). Extensions are version-controlled and deployed dynamically without disrupting core DT operations.

5. **Lifecycle Governance**
   - Governance is achieved through **semantic versioning** of DT artifacts, ensuring traceability and consistency across phases. The framework includes **role-based access control** (aligned with Table I roles) to manage permissions for Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, and DT Engineers. Additionally, **audit logs** and **change impact analysis** tools are embedded to monitor modifications and ensure compliance with operational requirements.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The twinning process is **model-driven**, leveraging BIM enrichment to embed **control semantics** (e.g., sensor-actuator-controller mappings) directly into building models. This phase adheres to **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles**, where enriched BIM models undergo **automated transformations** (M2M/M2T) to generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

**Quality Assurance and Testing**:
- The framework does **not explicitly mention formal testing methodologies** (e.g., unit tests, integration tests) in the provided documents. However, the **automated transformation pipeline** ensures structural consistency between BIM and DT representations, reducing manual errors.
- **Version control** is implied through **semantic versioning** of DT artifacts, enabling traceability and rollback capabilities.

**Evolution Management**:
- The DT evolves through **iterative improvements** in control policies, facilitated by the BOREALIS project. Custom services and plugins are integrated via **standardized extension points**, allowing DT Engineers to adapt functionality without disrupting core operations. The modular architecture supports **incremental updates** to the twin’s analytical and control capabilities over time.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The framework provides **decision support capabilities** through **real-time monitoring** and **interactive visualization** of building operations, enabling Facility Managers to make data-driven decisions. Key components include:
- **Real-time data exchange** between physical and digital twins, synchronized via standardized integration frameworks (e.g., Eclipse Hono, Ditto).
- **Immersive visualization** using **Unreal Engine** for rich, interactive interfaces that allow stakeholders to monitor system states and operate infrastructure via the DT.
- **Simulation capabilities** for predictive analytics, though specific analytics tools (e.g., Apache Kafka, Spark) are not mentioned.

Decision-making processes rely on **monitored system states** and **control logic defined by Building Control Engineers**, with confidence measures derived from **synchronized data** between the physical and digital domains. User interaction is facilitated through **interactive DT interfaces**, but no specific mention of advanced analytics (e.g., Bayesian probabilities, SHAP values) or decision support tools (e.g., Tableau, MATLAB Simulink) is provided.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The framework adheres to **standardized integration frameworks** (e.g., Eclipse Hono, Ditto) for interoperability between BIM models and DT artifacts. While specific industry standards (e.g., IFC4, BACnet, OPC UA) are not explicitly listed, the document emphasizes:
- **BIM as the foundational standard** for device modeling and control logic definition.
- **Modular architecture** with standardized extension points to support third-party integrations.
- **Real-time data exchange protocols** (implied via Eclipse Hono and Ditto) for synchronization between physical and digital twins.

No explicit mention of compliance frameworks (e.g., ISO/IEC 23247, GDPR, NIST SP 800-53) or protocol versions is provided. The framework’s interoperability is primarily enabled by **MDE-based transformations** and **standardized DT deployment artifacts**, ensuring consistency across the lifecycle.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the system as a 'Living Lab' and an 'academic building' and accurately references the 'LEED Platinum' certification attributed to the Gerber et al. case study. It also correctly identifies the TwinLight and BOREALIS projects as the research context. However, the specific physical components listed (structural/environmental sensors, BMS, interactive digital interfaces on the physical side) are not explicitly detailed in the source text; the text focuses on the software framework and general 'sensor data' rather than a physical component inventory. The extraction also attributes framework features (modular, extensible) to the physical system.

The extraction correctly identifies the target users (Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, DT Engineers) and general service categories like real-time monitoring and visualization using Unreal Engine. However, it fails by inventing specific technical methods (e.g., 'control charts, moving averages' and explicitly excluding 'Isolation Forest') and tools (e.g., 'Impromptu [40]', 'React.js + D3.js', 'AR overlays') that are not present in the source text. The description of 'Predictive Analytics' is largely speculative and unsupported by the provided snippets.

While the extraction correctly identifies key platforms mentioned in the text—Eclipse Hono, Eclipse Ditto, and Unreal Engine 5—and notes the avoidance of proprietary tools like BaSyx and Bentley iTwin.js, the majority of the listed technologies are unsupported. The source text does not mention Apache Kafka, PostgreSQL, TimescaleDB, Eclipse Modeling Framework (EMF), Acceleo, Modelica, React.js, Spring Boot, GitLab, or standards like OPC UA and ISO 15926. The extraction relies heavily on outside knowledge or speculation to populate the tooling list.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_2_judge.reasoning: The description accurately reflects the system's focus on 'real-time data exchange' and 'live synchronization' between physical and digital counterparts. It correctly identifies the use of 'runtime configuration management' for dynamic needs and 'interactive visualization' via Unreal Engine. It also correctly notes that specific synchronization frequencies are not detailed in the text.

The extraction precisely identifies the centralized 'hub-and-spoke' architecture and the three specific actor roles (Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, DT Engineers) defined in Table I. It correctly notes that the document does not describe decentralized or parallel twin instances, accurately reflecting the content scope.

The extraction provides a very good description of the system's orchestration, correctly identifying the 'centralized hub-and-spoke architecture' where the 'DT Framework acts as a central hub'. It accurately lists the functions like 'bidirectional data exchange' and 'Runtime configuration management'. It perfectly matches the source's listing of 'nonfunctional requirements (NF_X)' including 'interoperability', 'scalability', 'maintainability', and 'accessibility'. The description is highly specific and strongly grounded in the provided text.

The extraction correctly identifies the specific tools used for integration (Eclipse Hono and Ditto) and the goal of real-time data exchange. _•_ **R2:** An automated deployment process that reduces manual intervention and enables reproducible DT setups ( **C2** ).

_•_ **R3:** Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and
Ditto, enabling live synchronization between physical
assets and digital twins ( **C3** ).

_•_ **R4:** A modular architecture with defined extension points
to support scalable, service-oriented DT development in
AECO ( **C4** ).

Together, these contributions aim to operationalize scalable
and interoperable DTs in AECO contexts. By closing the gap
between design-time models and runtime operations, we offer
a sustainable path toward domain-wide DT adoption.
Our work is organized using Design Science Research
(DSR) [59] and provides artifacts to facilitate model-driven deployment of operational control DTs in the AECO domain. We
start with requirements elicitation (cf. Section III-A) and conclude with prototyping and experimentation (cf. Section V).



Our artifacts are developed in response to the following design
science problem [59]:


**Improve** _DT_ _deployment_ _in_ _BIM-based_ _infrastructure_
_projects_ (context)
**by designing** _a model-driven automation framework_ (artifact)
**that satisfies** _semantic integration, real-time connectivity, and_
_extensibility_ (requirement)
**in order to** _enable scalable DT engineering in the AECO_
_domain_ . (goal)


III. BIM- BASED D IGITAL T WINS

BIM is increasingly regarded as the principal modelling
paradigm in the AECO sector, providing a comprehensive
framework for generating, managing, and disseminating digital
building information throughout the entire building life cycle
with policy initiatives and regulatory mandates decisively
fostering and accelerating this development [60]. BIM enables
the creation of semantically rich, object-based 3D models that
incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics
of building components and their relationships. These models provide a collaborative platform where architects, engineers, and construction professionals can coordinate design
decisions, detect clashes, simulate performance, and generate
documentation [61].
While there are also DTs and industry-specific solutions
for the digital mapping of assets in other industries, such
as the manufacturing industry (e.g. capitalizing on Modelbased Systems Engineering; MBSE) [1], [62], BIM is becoming the standard solution in the construction industry [63],

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
